edges.description: FGE deficiency prevents normal FGly-dependent activation of newly synthesized sulfatases.
Failed FGly-dependent activation produces simultaneous deficiency of multiple sulfatase activities.
Decreased sulfatase activities impair degradation of glycosaminoglycans and sulfatides.
Impaired degradation of sulfated glycosaminoglycans leads to urinary mucopolysaccharide excretion.

intermediate_mechanisms: sulfated substrate storage in nervous-system cells
sulfated substrate storage in sensory tissues

edges.description: Visceral lysosomal storage contributes to liver enlargement.
Visceral lysosomal storage contributes to spleen enlargement.
Sulfatase deficiency and sulfated lipid/substrate storage produce the ichthyotic skin phenotype.
Hair changes are grouped with cutaneous storage manifestations.
Mucopolysaccharidosis-like storage produces coarse craniofacial features.
Smooth philtrum is retained as a craniofacial manifestation with unresolved proximal mechanism.
Anteverted nares is retained as a craniofacial manifestation with unresolved proximal mechanism.
Thick eyebrows are grouped with coarse craniofacial and hair manifestations.
Depressed nasal bridge is retained as a craniofacial manifestation with unresolved proximal mechanism.
Astrocyte and neuroglial dysfunction drives progressive neuronal loss.
Hydrocephalus is connected through central nervous system involvement, but the proximal mechanism is not separately modeled.
Microcephaly is modeled as a CNS growth/development manifestation of severe neurodevelopmental disease.
Macrocephaly is modeled as a CNS growth or hydrocephalus-related manifestation with unresolved proximal mechanism.
Cataract is modeled as an ocular manifestation of MSD, with the proximal tissue mechanism not separately curated.
Corneal opacity is modeled as an ocular storage manifestation with unresolved proximal mechanism.
Skeletal storage and dysplasia contribute to impaired linear growth.

nodes.description: AAV9/SUMF1 gene replacement is a preclinical strategy designed to supply functional SUMF1 and restore sulfatase activity; evidence is currently from mouse and rat preclinical studies rather than clinical use.

evidence.reference_title: Preclinical use of a clinically-relevant scAAV9/SUMF1 vector for the treatment of multiple sulfatase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: treatment of neonates extends survival up to 1-year post-injection.

evidence.reference_title: Preclinical use of a clinically-relevant scAAV9/SUMF1 vector for the treatment of multiple sulfatase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The treated mice show wide distribution of the SUMF1 gene, no signs of toxicity or neuropathy, improved vision and cardiac function, and no behavioral deficits.
HP:0003134 | Abnormality of peripheral nerve conduction | Very frequent (99-80%)
HP:0007703 | Abnormality of retinal pigmentation | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency from an Ophthalmologist's Perspective-Case Report and Literature Review.
evidence.snippet: The main pathology these patients present is a highly conspicuous tapetoretinal degeneration, similar to severe Retinitis pigmentosa, that leads to blindness at an early age.

evidence.snippet: loss of sulfatase activity induces lysosomal storage of glycosaminoglycans and sulfatides and subsequent cellular pathology.

evidence.reference_title: A systematic cross-sectional survey of multiple sulfatase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: FGE deficiency results in widespread tissue accumulation of multiple sulphated substrates.

nodes.description: Hematopoietic cell or bone marrow transplantation is investigational in MSD; a two-sibling attenuated-MSD case series reports correction of available biomarkers and possible slowing of disease progression, with longer follow-up needed.

evidence.reference_title: Bone marrow transplantation reverses metabolic alterations in multiple sulfatase deficiency: a case series.
evidence.snippet: There is evidence of correction of currently available biomarkers within 3 months post-HCT.

evidence.reference_title: Bone marrow transplantation reverses metabolic alterations in multiple sulfatase deficiency: a case series.
evidence.snippet: These data suggest biochemical benefits post-transplant along with slowing of disease progression.

nodes.description: Neuroglial dysfunction produces progressive neuronal loss and central and peripheral nervous-system degeneration, manifesting as leukodystrophy-like disease, developmental impairment, regression, seizures, and peripheral nerve involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency: A Disease Comprising Mucopolysaccharidosis, Sphingolipidosis, and More Caused by a Defect in Posttranslational Modification.
evidence.snippet: impairing astrocyte function. This led to neuronal loss thereby highlighting the importance of astrocyte dysfunction in the pathophysiology of MSD

evidence.reference_title: A systematic cross-sectional survey of multiple sulfatase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: MSD is an ultra-rare multisystem disorder with mainly neurologic, hearing and skin involvements.

nodes.description: Sulfatide and glycosaminoglycan storage in neurons and glia — particularly astrocytes — impairs lysosomal and autophagic function, compromising the cellular processes that maintain nervous-system homeostasis.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency: A Disease Comprising Mucopolysaccharidosis, Sphingolipidosis, and More Caused by a Defect in Posttranslational Modification.
evidence.snippet: This abnormal storage results in lysosomal dysfunction, impacting several cellular processes including autophagy

evidence.snippet: Astrocyte specific knock-out of SUMF1 in a Cre/Lox MSD mouse model revealed lysosomal storage of substrates and autophagy substrates impairing astrocyte function.

evidence.snippet: Historically, the clinical diagnosis of MSD was confirmed by measuring simultaneous deficiency of more than one sulfatase activity in patient leukocytes or fibroblasts.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency: A Disease Comprising Mucopolysaccharidosis, Sphingolipidosis, and More Caused by a Defect in Posttranslational Modification.
evidence.snippet: MSD is caused by impaired posttranslational activation of sulfatases through the formylglycine generating enzyme (FGE) encoded by the sulfatase modifying factor 1 (SUMF1) gene, which is mutated in MSD.

evidence.reference_title: A systematic cross-sectional survey of multiple sulfatase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency (MSD) is an inborn error of metabolism caused by pathogenic variants in the SUMF1 gene encoding the formylglycine-generating enzyme (FGE) that activates all known sulfatases.

nodes.description: Pathogenic SUMF1 variants reduce formylglycine-generating enzyme stability or catalytic activity in the endoplasmic reticulum.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple sulfatase deficiency is caused by mutations in the gene encoding the human C(alpha)-formylglycine generating enzyme.
evidence.snippet: C(alpha)-formylglycine (FGly) is the catalytic residue in the active site of eukaryotic sulfatases.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple sulfatase deficiency is caused by mutations in the gene encoding the human C(alpha)-formylglycine generating enzyme.
evidence.snippet: We purified the FGly generating enzyme (FGE) and identified its gene and nine mutations in seven MSD patients.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency: A Disease Comprising Mucopolysaccharidosis, Sphingolipidosis, and More Caused by a Defect in Posttranslational Modification.
evidence.snippet: SUMF1 mutations result in unstable, degradation-prone FGE that demonstrates reduced or absent catalytic activity, leading to decreased activity of all sulfatases.

nodes.description: Failed FGE-dependent formylglycine generation lowers activity of multiple cellular sulfatases, including lysosomal sulfatases.

evidence.reference_title: The multiple sulfatase deficiency gene encodes an essential and limiting factor for the activity of sulfatases.
evidence.snippet: In multiple sulfatase deficiency (MSD), a human inherited disorder, the activities of all sulfatases are impaired due to a defect in posttranslational modification.

evidence.reference_title: The multiple sulfatase deficiency gene encodes an essential and limiting factor for the activity of sulfatases.
evidence.snippet: Coexpression of SUMF1 with sulfatases results in a strikingly synergistic increase of enzymatic activity, indicating that SUMF1 is both an essential and a limiting factor for sulfatases.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sulfatase Deficiency: A Disease Comprising Mucopolysaccharidosis, Sphingolipidosis, and More Caused by a Defect in Posttranslational Modification.
evidence.snippet: A complete loss of SUMF1 or FGE function results in decreased activity of all sulfatases rendering MSD a monogenetic disease
